findings.title: File descriptor leak on RequestedRangeNotSatisfiable when file was opened by send_file
findings.detail: When `file is None` before the range-request block (i.e. send_file opened the file itself at line 549), the local variable `file` holds an open file handle. On a 416 error `make_conditional` raises `RequestedRangeNotSatisfiable`. The handler at line 594 does `file.close()` — but this only works if `file` is still the object that was opened. However, when `use_x_sendfile` is True, `data = None` and `file` may have been closed already (line 542). Conversely, when file was passed in by the *caller* (the `else` branch at line 516-518), calling `file.close()` here is wrong: the caller owns that file object and closing it without the caller's knowledge is a bug. The code should only close the file if it was opened internally (i.e. `filename` was provided and `use_x_sendfile` is False).

findings.title: fsize is None when a file object is passed, causing make_conditional to receive None as complete_length
findings.detail: When `filename_or_fp` is a file-like object (not a string), `fsize` stays `None` (it is only set in the `use_x_sendfile` branch or the `file is None` branch). The new code path at line 591-592 then calls `rv.make_conditional(request, accept_ranges=True, complete_length=None)`. Passing `None` as `complete_length` may cause incorrect or undefined behaviour in Werkzeug's range-request handling (e.g. it cannot generate a valid `Content-Range` header). For file objects that support `seek`/`tell`, the size could be computed; otherwise range requests should be skipped or the Accept-Ranges header should not be advertised.

findings.title: Range requests silently unsupported / no Accept-Ranges header when file object is passed
findings.detail: When a file-like object (not a filename string) is passed, `fsize` is `None`. The call to `make_conditional(request, accept_ranges=True, complete_length=None)` will attempt to advertise `Accept-Ranges: bytes` and process a Range header without knowing the total size. Per RFC 7233, a server must know the complete length to emit a valid `Content-Range`. The code should either compute the size (via seek/tell) or fall back to the old `make_conditional(request)` path (no range support) when the size is unknown.

findings.title: file variable may be undefined when RequestedRangeNotSatisfiable is raised with use_x_sendfile
findings.detail: In the `use_x_sendfile` branch (line 540-546), `data = None` and the original file object (if any) was already closed (line 542). After that, `file` may be the original caller-supplied file (now closed) or the local `file` variable which was set to `None` at line 513 (for the filename-only path). When `make_conditional` raises in the X-Sendfile path, calling `file.close()` at line 594 would either close an already-closed file or call `close()` on `None` (AttributeError). The guard `if file is not None: file.close()` is missing.

findings.title: Test typo in skip reason: 'not implement' should be 'not implemented'
findings.detail: The `reason` string in the `@pytest.mark.skipif` decorator reads `"not implement within werkzeug"` — it should be `"not implemented within werkzeug"` (missing 'd').

findings.title: No test for range request when file-like object (not filename) is passed
findings.detail: The new range-request logic has a code path for file-like objects where `fsize` is None. There is no test exercising this case. Given the potential issue with `complete_length=None`, a test covering a file-object input with `conditional=True` and a `Range` header would help catch regressions.

findings.title: No test verifying Accept-Ranges header is present in response
findings.detail: RFC 7233 requires that a server advertising range-request support include `Accept-Ranges: bytes` in its responses. The new tests check status codes and data content but do not verify that the `Accept-Ranges` response header is set correctly on a plain (non-range) GET request.

findings.title: Version-detection via duck-typing on Range class is fragile and adds complexity
findings.detail: Using `callable(getattr(Range, 'to_content_range_header', None))` to detect Werkzeug version support is an unusual pattern. A cleaner approach would be to explicitly check the Werkzeug version via `werkzeug.__version__` or use a try/except import of the new API. The comment says 'Remove this test when support for Werkzeug <0.12 is dropped', but it is easy to forget. Consider using `pkg_resources` version comparison or a module-level boolean sentinel set once at import time, rather than repeating the `getattr` test on every request.

findings.title: 304 x-sendfile cleanup not applied to 416 responses
findings.detail: The existing comment says x-sendfile headers should be stripped for 304 responses. With range requests, a 416 response could theoretically also be returned; but since 416 is re-raised as an exception, the cleanup never runs for that case. This is a minor inconsistency but worth noting — an X-Sendfile header could linger if a future Werkzeug version returns 416 as a response object rather than raising.
